05 Business Process Mapping - YouTube.mp4
Whether you're mapping a growth, fulfillment, or innovation engine, the process is more or less the same. But first, you need to understand the basic symbols of business process mapping. And they are... The Pill? The square, or rectangle, The diamond! The parallelogram, and the line. The pill shape, also referred to as the terminus, represents the start or end of any process map. The square or rectangle represents a task or activity. The diamond represents a decision point or gateway, and these are used anytime there's a fork in the road where a process can go one way or another, just depending on the actions or circumstances of the participant that's moving through a given process map. So, for example, if you're mapping a growth engine and one of the stages is a registration page, obviously the customer can either choose to register or not register.
If they register, they'll be sent one direction, and if they don't register, they'll either be sent another direction or the process will end. Either way, if the step is not a direct, linear process, you have reached a decision point, which is represented by that diamond shape. The parallelogram is used to denote a data input or export. For example, if you want to show that a report of findings or the results of a questionnaire is a particular stage in a process, then you would denote that with a parallelogram since it's not an action or activity, but rather an asset that is either into the process flow or exported from the process flow. Now, I want to be clear on this. This is the least used symbol.
So if you find that you rarely or even never use it, don't worry about it. Finally, we have the line, which simply connects the other elements and shows the flow through a given process map. Now, I wanna give you a bit of a disclaimer here. When you're mapping your value engines, it's common for stages to be added, deleted, or moved around. And so for that reason, we suggest waiting until the last possible moment to draw your lines and connect the dots. Because if you do it too soon, you're going to have a lot of erasing to do. So just to put it into context, the pill shape is the terminus. And it's used to denote the start and end of a process map. The square represents tasks and activities in a given process.
The diamond is used to denote a gateway or decision point, basically any time the process forks in two or more directions, and the lines connect all the squares and diamonds to and from the start and end termini, just to show how the whole system flows together. Now, I realize that, as we're talking about this right now, it may seem a little bit complicated, but please stick with me. When you take it one step at a time, which we're going to show you how to do, value engines kind of wind up mapping themselves. So now that you understand the basic symbols, let's map your value engines.
